title: Italic capital letters — chancery hand A to Z
subtitle: The broad-edge italic majuscules at 45 degrees nib angle
excerpt: Italic capitals are the natural companion to the italic minuscule hand. They share the same 45-degree nib angle and the same elliptical compression but stand 7 to 8 nib widths tall and carry optional serifs. This tutorial covers all 26 letters with stroke order.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapters on italic hands and versals. The italic capital system described here follows the chancery italic tradition documented in Johnston's analysis of sixteenth-century Italian writing masters. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

glossaryTerms.definition: A broad-edge capital letter in the chancery italic tradition, written at a 5 to 10 degree forward slope with compressed oval proportions. Typically 7 to 8 nib widths tall.
The angle of the broad-edge nib relative to the writing line. Italic capitals use 45 degrees, the same as the italic minuscule.
The height of capital letters, measured in nib widths. Italic capitals sit at 7 nib widths; roman capitals at 7 to 8.

content.text: The
italic capitals
used in chancery italic writing derive from the Roman lapidary capitals but are compressed slightly forward and narrowed to match the italic minuscule they pair with. The
nib angle
is 45 degrees throughout.
Cap height
is 7 nib widths. The letters slope 5 to 10 degrees forward from the vertical.
What you need
Ruling the guide lines

Set two pairs of lines: baseline and cap-height line (7 nib widths above), with an x-height line 4.5 nib widths above the baseline to serve as a reference for the branching height of mixed text. Rule a slope guide at 7 degrees forward from the vertical if you want consistent forward lean across the sheet.
Grouped letter forms
Round letters: O, C, G, Q

O sets the proportions for the group. Italic O is a compressed oval, not a circle: the width is roughly 75% of the height. Two strokes: stroke 1 sweeps from 11 o'clock down and left to the baseline; stroke 2 closes the right side from 1 o'clock down and right to the baseline, meeting stroke 1 at both ends. C is stroke 1 of O without the closing stroke. G is C with a short horizontal ledge at mid-height. Q is O with a short diagonal tail descending from the lower right of the bowl.
Straight-sided letters: I, H, L, T, F, E

I is a single downstroke from cap height to baseline, with an optional entry serif at the top and an exit serif at the base. H has two downstrokes connected by a crossbar at mid-height. T has a single downstroke with a horizontal arm at the top (2 to 3 nib widths on each side of the stem). F is T without the base serif, the arm slightly shorter on the right. E adds a mid-height arm and a base arm to L.
Diagonal letters: V, W, X, Y, Z, A, K, M, N

V is two diagonal strokes meeting at a pointed base: stroke 1 goes from cap height down-right to the midpoint of the baseline; stroke 2 goes from cap height down-left to meet it. W is two V units side by side, sharing the middle upstroke. A is an inverted V with a crossbar: stroke 1 from top down-left to baseline, stroke 2 from top down-right, stroke 3 a horizontal bar at mid-height.
Arched letters: B, D, P, R, U, J

B has two bowls: a smaller upper bowl and a larger lower bowl, both attached to the left-side stem. The upper bowl fills the top half; the lower bowl fills the bottom half and extends slightly beyond the width of the upper. P is a single upright with a half-round upper bowl only. R is P with a leg: the leg kicks out to the right from the point where the bowl meets the stem.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: Errors in italic capitals
attrs.intro: Problems that appear when learning the italic capitals alongside the minuscule.

items.symptom: Capitals look heavier and wider than the minuscule letters they accompany
items.cause: Italic capitals are often written at the same nib width as the minuscule but at 8 nib widths height, making them proportionally taller and denser.
items.fix: Try the capitals at 7 nib widths height. Also narrow the letterforms so the O is visibly oval rather than circular.

items.symptom: Serifs on the capitals look different from letter to letter
items.cause: Serifs on broad-edge italics are formed by a small entry flick or exit press; inconsistency in the entry direction causes variation.
items.fix: Decide at the start of a piece whether to use hairline entry serifs, slab serifs, or no serifs. Use the same choice throughout.
